original_abstract: A redox-active metal-organic framework, Fe2(dobpdc) (dobpdc(4-) = 4,4'-dioxidobiphenyl-3,3'-dicarboxylate), is shown to undergo a topotactic oxidative insertion reaction with a variety of weakly coordinating anions, including BF4(-) and PF6(-). The reaction results in just a minor lattice contraction, and a broad intervalence charge-transfer band emerges, indicative of charge mobility. Although both metal- and ligand-based oxidations can be accessed, only the former were found to be fully reversible and, importantly, proceed stoichiometrically under both chemical and electrochemical conditions. Electrochemical measurements probing the effects of nanoconfinement on the insertion reaction revealed strong anion size and solvent dependences. Significantly, the anion insertion behavior of Fe2(dobpdc) enabled its use in the construction of a dual-ion battery prototype incorporating a sodium anode. As a cathode, the material displays a particularly high initial reduction potential and is further stable for at least 50 charge/discharge cycles, exhibiting a maximum specific energy of 316 Wh/kg.
humanized_abstract: The redox-active metal-organic framework (MOF) Fe2(dobpdc) (dobpdc(4-) denotes 4,4'-dioxidobiphenyl-3,3'-dicarboxylate) underwent topotactic oxidative insertion reaction by oxidizing with a variety of weakly coordinating anions, including BF4(-) and PF6(-). The insertion reaction led to only a little shrinkage of framework, and the appearance of broad intervalence charge-transfer band in UV-Vis spectra, indicating good charge transportation. While both metal and ligand-based oxidations were accessible, the former was fully reversible, and more importantly, all of these oxidations proceeded in a stoichiometric manner under both chemical and electrochemical conditions. Investigation on electrochemical properties of the insertion reaction in the framework under nanoconfinement showed strong dependences on the sizes of anions and solvents. On the basis of anion insertion properties of the Fe2(dobpdc), a dual-ion battery was constructed using sodium as the anode electrode. In this cathode, the Fe2(dobpdc) electrode shows high initial reduction potential and a high cycling stability for at least 50 charge/discharge cycles, delivering a maximum specific energy of up to 316 Wh/kg.

While both metal and ligand-based oxidations were accessible, the former was fully reversible, and more importantly, all of these oxidations proceeded in a stoichiometric manner under both chemical and electrochemical conditions. document.input: A redox-active metal-organic framework, Fe2(dobpdc) (dobpdc(4-) = 4,4'-dioxidobiphenyl-3,3'-dicarboxylate), is shown to undergo a topotactic oxidative insertion reaction with a variety of weakly coordinating anions, including BF4(-) and PF6(-). The reaction results in just a minor lattice contraction, and a broad intervalence charge-transfer band emerges, indicative of charge mobility. Although both metal- and ligand-based oxidations can be accessed, only the former were found to be fully reversible and, importantly, proceed stoichiometrically under both chemical and electrochemical conditions. Electrochemical measurements probing the effects of nanoconfinement on the insertion reaction revealed strong anion size and solvent dependences. As a cathode, the material displays a particularly high initial reduction potential and is further stable for at least 50 charge/discharge cycles, exhibiting a maximum specific energy of 316 Wh/kg.
